title: Quarter-fold A6 zine from two A4 sheets
subtitle: Making a 16-page A6 booklet with saddle-stitch binding from two sheets
excerpt: A 16-page A6 zine made from two A4 sheets and saddle-stitched down the centre is the standard home-production format. The pages are arranged so that each A4 sheet, when folded and inserted into the other, forms a complete 16-page booklet with the correct page sequence. The page imposition, the arrangement of pages on the flat sheet, is the only part that requires thought.
sourceNotes: The saddle-stitched pamphlet format is documented in bookbinding manuals from the nineteenth century and is the basis of all magazine and pamphlet printing. The specific A4-to-A6 imposition used here is a standard home-printing workflow for zine production.

glossaryTerms.definition: A binding method in which staples or thread are driven through the fold of a nested set of pages. The booklet is opened to its centre (draped over a 'saddle') and the staples go through from cover to centre. The simplest binding for thin pamphlets and zines.

The arrangement of pages on a flat printed sheet so that when the sheet is folded and cut, the pages appear in the correct reading order. For a 16-page saddle-stitched zine, the two flat sheets must each carry 8 pages in a specific arrangement.
One folded sheet that forms a section of a booklet. In a two-sheet 16-page zine, each sheet is one signature (4 pages when folded to A5, 8 pages when folded to A6).

content.text: is the logic puzzle at the heart of zine-making. For a 16-page A6 zine, fold each A4 sheet in half twice (to A6) and number the folded pages 1 to 16 in pencil to confirm the page sequence. Unfold and map which content goes where on the flat sheet. Once understood, the same imposition serves every future zine of the same format.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, two A4 sheets per copy
items.substitutions: 80 gsm printer paper for a lighter, smaller zine (fewer layers to staple through)

items.name: Long-arm stapler (reaches to centre of A4 sheet)
items.substitutions: Japanese stab binding (sew through) for a more durable alternative to stapling

content.text: Take a blank sheet. Fold in half across the short axis (landscape to portrait), then fold in half again across the short axis. You now have a small A6 booklet of 8 pages.
Number each page 1 to 8 in pencil, front to back. Unfold and note which page number is in each position on the flat A4 sheet.
Repeat for the second sheet, numbering 9 to 16. The second sheet will be nested inside the first.
Fold each printed sheet to A6, using a bone folder to sharpen each crease. Work with the fold from the centre out to prevent the paper from shifting.
Open both folded sheets flat. Nest
2 (pages 9-16) inside signature 1 (pages 1-8), aligning the spine folds.

Open the nested sheets to the centre spread. Place the long-arm stapler along the spine fold. Drive two staples through the fold, one approximately 10 mm from the top edge, one 10 mm from the bottom edge.
Close the booklet and press the spine firmly with the bone folder to flatten the staple clinches.

items.symptom: Pages are in the wrong order
items.cause: Imposition was not worked out before printing, or the wrong sheet was nested inside.
items.fix: Always work out the imposition on a blank dummy copy first. Mark the sheet numbers before printing.

items.symptom: The stapler will not reach the spine fold
items.cause: Using a standard stapler rather than a long-arm stapler.
items.fix: Use a long-arm saddle-stitch stapler. Alternative: use a bookbinding awl to pre-pierce the spine at two points, then sew with linen thread (Japanese stab binding variation).
